Who I Work With

Four types of organizations. One thing in common.

The challenge looks different depending on where you sit — but it comes from the same place. The organization has outgrown what its current leadership infrastructure can support.

Operations

CEOs & Founders whose org is scaling faster than its structure

Priorities owned by no one. The CEO in every meeting. Transformation that launches and quietly reverts. You need an operational executive who builds the system that runs without you — and stays accountable to results, not a deck.

→ Executive Ops · PMO · Transformation

People & Culture

CHROs & CPOs who need an embedded people operations partner

Engagement slipping. Communications reactive. Succession planning nonexistent. ER issues piling up with no one to own them. You need someone who has built the whole people operating model — investigations, policies, ERGs, comms — not just advised on it.

→ HR · ER · Engagement · Succession · Comms

Customer Success

CCOs & CROs whose CS function isn't returning what it costs

Retention inconsistent. NPS declining. The CS team is working hard without the lifecycle frameworks or governance to deliver at scale. You need a CS architect who has sustained 98-99% retention across 300+ enterprise accounts — and built the model that made it possible.

→ CS Architecture · Retention · NRR Growth

Transformation

Organizations navigating M&A, redesign, or major strategic change

The strategy is sound. But there's no one accountable for the execution layer — the place between decision and delivery where most transformations fail. You need a fractional executive who runs the execution, not a consultant who documents it.

→ M&A Integration · Change Management · OCM

About

The only fractional executive who started in HR, crossed into enterprise technology, and never stopped doing both.

"The only fractional executive who started in HR, crossed into enterprise technology operations, and never stopped doing both — for 25 years."

I'm Kim Arnold. I began my career as an HR professional — recruiting, generalist work, building college recruiting programs, mentoring frameworks, and new hire orientation from the ground up. When the company I worked for was acquired, my internal IT clients pulled me into their world — and I spent the rest of my career doing both simultaneously.

That crossover is everything. I don't bring operations expertise with a side of HR awareness. I bring deep formal foundations in both — and enterprise proof that they work together.

The career arc most executives don't have: Formal HR practitioner → enterprise IT operations → Chief of Staff for a 3,000-person global org → Director-level P&L ownership across four countries → fractional executive and available for the right permanent role. Every role reinforced both sides. That's why I can walk into a leadership team and own the operating model and the people model at the same time — whether it's fractional or full-time.

Tell Dell Rockstar — 15 of 17 years. Dell's top employee satisfaction designation for people leaders. Voluntary team attrition under 1% across 17 years and 140+ team members hired and developed. 16 team members promoted into people management roles.
01
You get results faster

I've held the Chief of Staff and Director seats — so there's no ramp-up teaching me how your world works. We diagnose the real problem in week one and start building immediately.

02
The work sticks after I leave

Most consulting engagements produce a deck. I build operating models your team owns and runs long after the engagement ends — because I build them with you, not for you.

03
Your leadership team finally has breathing room

When accountability is clear, decisions stop recycling, and the org moves without the CEO in every meeting — that's the goal. Every engagement is designed to give leadership back their time.

04
One person who covers the whole operating picture

Operations, people, culture, HR, and customer success — most organizations hire three different consultants for what I bring as one embedded fractional executive. That's a structural advantage.

Chief of Staff HR Generalist ER Investigations Director of CS PMO Director Operations Director Transformation Lead Workforce Planning M&A Integration People Communications

How I Engage

Seven ways a fractional executive changes what's possible.

Every engagement is tailored — never packaged. I work as a true fractional executive: embedded in your leadership team, accountable to outcomes, and designed to transfer ownership when the work is done. Available as retainer, project, or advisory.

Operating cadences, decision rights, accountability architecture — so the org moves without the CEO in every meeting. Governance that creates visibility without bureaucracy.

People, Culture & HR Operations

Employee relations investigations, policy development, HRBP partnership, ERG strategy, succession planning, engagement campaigns, internal communications, workforce planning, and onboarding design. The full people operating model — owned, not advised.

Workforce Strategy & Talent Architecture

Workforce planning, recruiting frameworks, contingent staffing models, hiring pipelines, and onboarding programs built for scale. Built from scratch at 15-person startups and 3,000-person global orgs alike.

Workforce · Talent

Customer Success Architecture

Lifecycle frameworks, onboarding models, retention playbooks, VOC programs, NRR/GRR inspection models, and CS team operating structures — built to turn CS into a revenue engine at enterprise scale.

Scalable Operating Model Design

For organizations growing faster than their infrastructure can support. Org structure, accountability model, and operational discipline redesigned for the next stage — not the last one.

How I Work

A structure built together,
not handed over.

The order matters. Skip diagnosis and you design the wrong thing. Skip execution and you get a deck. Skip embed and the structure doesn't hold.

01
Diagnose

Deep listening first. Where decisions stall, where accountability is unclear, where structure is outpacing the org. No assumptions.

02
Design

A tailored operating structure — governance, meeting architecture, accountability models, and KPIs built for your actual stage and culture.

03
Execute

In the work, not above it. Leading implementation, managing resistance, adjusting in real time. Accountable to results — not a deliverable.

04
Embed

Ownership transfers intentionally. The structure holds long after the engagement ends. That's the only real measure of success.

Case Study

Post-merger org in year six.
Still broken.

Dell Technologies · Chief of Staff, People Culture & Communications · 2022–2023

Six years after a major acquisition, a 3,000-person global Customer Success organization was still running as two companies. What this engagement looked like from the inside, and what it took to fix it in twelve months.

Post-Merger Integration Chief of Staff People & Culture Transformation Org Design
01The Situation

Six years into a high-profile acquisition, the organization was still visibly fractured. Two legacy cultures were operating in parallel, each with its own norms, informal hierarchies, and institutional loyalties. Tools weren't integrated. Job titles and compensation bands had been force-fitted: roles from the acquired company mapped to existing structures based on salary, not skill match, creating widespread title misalignment and comp inequity that people felt every day.

Without centralized ownership, the org had developed a pattern of self-help: when something wasn't working, someone would launch a new committee, a new program, a new process — without governance or coordination. The result was compounding redundancy. A relatively new SVP inherited all of it, without a consistent voice, communications infrastructure, or way to reach her 3,000-person global team.

The problems weren't invisible to leadership. They were visible to clients.

02What I Did

Rebuilt job architecture from the ground up — developed unified SOPs, reconciled job descriptions, and aligned comp bands and title structures across both legacy systems. Mandated performance assessments across all people leaders, enforced structured accountability, and initiated a RIF process targeting chronic underperformance that had been tolerated for years.

Consolidated the ERG structures from both legacy companies into a single unified model — expanded participation across all eight ERGs. Designed Dell's first-ever global Sustainability and Ethics campaign for the Services org. Built centralized communications infrastructure across Microsoft Teams and SharePoint — giving the entire 3,000-person org consistent messaging from their SVP for the first time since the acquisition.

Established governance before adding resources: defined ownership, accountability, and decision rights for every active program. Stopped the pattern of self-launched committees by replacing it with a visible, structured operating model the SVP could stand behind.

03The Results
30%
Increase in operational efficiency within 12 months
15%
Improvement in employee retention
1 yr
To move the org's Employee Satisfaction survey measurably

One unified org. One voice. One set of standards. The SVP had a communications platform, a governance model, and a people infrastructure she could run — and a team that finally knew what was expected of them. Employee Satisfaction scores for the group improved within the single year I held the role. The work that typically takes a three-year transformation program was delivered in twelve months by one embedded executive accountable for the outcome.
